title: Iran Deal Awaits Trump's Approval as Israel Deepens Lebanon Offensive
subhead: US and Iranian negotiators reached a tentative 60-day truce agreement to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, but President Trump has delayed his 'final determination' as Israel expands ground operations past the Litani River in southern Lebanon.

sections.heading: US-Iran Deal on the Brink as Trump Postpones Decision
sections.body:
US and Iranian negotiators have reached a tentative agreement to extend the ceasefire and begin new nuclear talks, according to multiple reports from CNN, Al Jazeera, and The New York Times. The deal would reportedly involve a 60-day truce during which Iran would reopen the Strait of Hormuz to commercial shipping and the US would lift its naval blockade of Iranian ports.

However, President Trump ended a meeting with national security advisers on Thursday without announcing a 'final determination' on whether to approve the proposal. Trump told reporters he would make a decision 'soon' and reiterated that 'no one will control the Strait of Hormuz' — maintaining his hardline public stance even as diplomatic progress continues behind closed doors.

Secretary of State Marco Rubio and other officials described 'slight progress' in talks, with UAE presidential advisor Anwar Gargash putting the odds of a deal at '50-50.' The central sticking point remains whether Iran will fully relinquish control over Hormuz transits without face-saving concessions on sanctions relief.
sections.whyItMatters: This is the closest the US and Iran have come to ending the three-month war that has disrupted global oil flows and redrawn alliance maps in the Middle East. Trump's delay suggests he is weighing domestic political costs against the economic benefits of reopening Hormuz. A signed deal would dramatically reduce tensions but would also require verification mechanisms that Tehran may resist — and would leave unresolved the underlying nuclear questions that preceded the conflict.

sections.heading: Israel Crosses Litani River as Lebanon Death Toll Mounts
sections.body:
Israeli ground forces have crossed the Litani River in southern Lebanon, expanding their offensive beyond the UN-patrolled security zone for the first time since the conflict began. Reuters and The Washington Post report that Israeli troops are pushing deeper into Lebanese territory despite ongoing ceasefire talks between the US and Iran — the third front in Israel's multi-theater war.

Lebanon's health ministry reports more than 3,100 people killed by Israeli attacks since March 2, with 9,432 injured. Israeli strikes continue on Beirut's southern suburbs and southern Lebanese towns, with evacuation orders expanding to cover large swaths of territory near the front lines.

Lebanese President Joseph Aoun told Secretary of State Marco Rubio that a ceasefire is 'essential,' while Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu vowed to 'crush' Hezbollah regardless of the Iran negotiations. The disconnect between Washington's diplomatic push and Jerusalem's military escalation underscores the limits of US leverage over its ally.
sections.whyItMatters: Israel's offensive is proceeding independently of the US-Iran track, suggesting that even if Hormuz reopens, the Lebanon front will continue. Hezbollah's rejection of ceasefire frameworks and Israel's determination to eliminate the militia's capabilities create a two-track reality: diplomatic progress on Iran while fighting grinds on in Lebanon. The death toll and displacement are creating long-term humanitarian consequences that will outlast any US-Iran agreement.

sections.heading: Russia Prepares 'Massive New Strike' as Ukraine War Grinds On
sections.body:
Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy warned that Russia is preparing a 'massive new strike' on Ukraine, citing intelligence reports of renewed missile and drone attacks. The warning comes as Ukrainian forces continue drone strikes on Russian infrastructure, including oil facilities in western Russia and a tanker in occupied Crimea, according to The Kyiv Independent.

Russian President Vladimir Putin said his recent comment that the Ukraine war was 'coming to an end' was based on 'battlefield advances' — a claim that contrasts with the grinding attrition on the front lines. Two civilians were killed in the Belgorod region by Ukrainian drone attacks, while Zelenskyy urged citizens to 'stay alert' amid the threat of renewed Russian bombardment.

A Russian drone strayed into NATO territory in Romania, injuring two people and prompting outcry from EU and NATO members. Putin deflected questions about the incident, saying it was 'too early' to determine the drone's origin.
sections.whyItMatters: The Ukraine war has receded from headlines as the Iran conflict dominates, but it continues to consume lives and resources on both sides. Russian drone incursions into NATO territory — whether accidental or deliberate — create escalation risks that could draw the alliance directly into the conflict. Zelenskyy's warning about a 'massive new strike' suggests the war's third summer will bring renewed intensity.

sections.heading: NATO Confronts Russian Incursions and US Commitment Questions
sections.body:
A Russian drone crash in Romania that injured two civilians has sparked discussions about invoking NATO's Article 4, which convenes alliance consultations when a member's territorial integrity is threatened. Romania's foreign minister said the country could invoke the provision, though NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte emphasized that the alliance is 'ready to defend every inch' of territory.

The incident comes amid continued uncertainty about US troop deployments in Europe. Trump's reversal on sending 5,000 troops to Poland — after previously canceling the same deployment — has left European allies 'bewildered,' according to AP reporting. Germany and the Netherlands announced plans to set up a NATO command center in the Baltics, reflecting European efforts to bolster defenses independently of Washington.

Rutte sought to reassure allies that US troop withdrawals would not 'hurt Europe,' but the confusion over deployments has accelerated discussions about Europe taking greater responsibility for its own defense — including potential Article 5 contingencies.
sections.whyItMatters: The Romania incident tests NATO's collective defense credibility at a moment when European confidence in US commitment is already shaken. If Russia continues to accidentally or deliberately breach NATO airspace, the alliance faces pressure to respond without triggering a wider war. European efforts to build independent defense capabilities are accelerating, but significant gaps remain — particularly in air defense, intelligence, and expeditionary logistics.

sections.heading: China Watches Middle East Chaos, Pressures Taiwan Quietly
sections.body:
As the Iran conflict dominates US attention, China continues pressure operations against Taiwan with less scrutiny. Taiwan's government suspects Nvidia chips are being smuggled to China via Japan, according to Bloomberg, raising concerns about sanctions enforcement and dual-use technology transfers. A new Taiwan-Japan ferry service debuted on a ship designed for wartime evacuation, highlighting contingency planning in Taipei.

The Institute for the Study of War's daily China-Taiwan updates continue to track PLA activity around the island, with recent assessments noting 'no significant changes' in military posture. Analysts suggest Beijing is calibrating its approach — maintaining pressure without escalating while the US is distracted by the Middle East.

Japan is reassessing its China policy, with debates ongoing about whether to explicitly label China a 'threat' in key government documents. The Trump administration's opening to Beijing during the Iran crisis has created anxiety in Tokyo and Taipei about potential US concessions at their expense.
sections.whyItMatters: The Middle East conflict has drawn US military and diplomatic resources away from the Indo-Pacific, creating opportunities for China to advance its interests with reduced pushback. Taiwan and Japan are watching the Iran negotiations closely, concerned that any US-China understanding might involve trade-offs on issues like Taiwan arms sales or technology restrictions. The chip smuggling allegations underscore how technology controls are tested even as geopolitical alignments shift.

bottomLine: Day 91 of the Iran war brings the closest moment yet to a potential ceasefire, with US and Iranian negotiators reaching a tentative deal that now sits on President Trump's desk awaiting approval. The agreement would extend the truce by 60 days, reopen Hormuz, and open the door to new nuclear negotiations — but Trump's public insistence that 'no one will control' the strait and his delay in announcing a decision suggest internal debates over terms and political costs. Meanwhile, Israel's ground offensive in Lebanon has crossed the Litani River, killing over 3,100 people and displacing hundreds of thousands, with no end in sight regardless of what happens in US-Iran talks. In Europe, a Russian drone strike on NATO territory in Romania has triggered discussions of Article 4 consultations, testing alliance cohesion at a moment when European confidence in US commitment remains shaky. Ukraine's Zelenskyy warns of a 'massive new strike' from Russia, a reminder that the world's wars are not pausing while Washington focuses on Tehran. And in the Indo-Pacific, China continues its quiet pressure on Taiwan, watching US distraction with strategic patience. The coming days will determine whether the Hormuz deal holds or the Middle East spirals into deeper conflict — but even a signed agreement would leave multiple wars burning elsewhere.
